text: When Isaac was old and his eyes too dim to see, he called Esau, his older son, who answered, here I am.
Isaac said he was old and did not know the day of his death.
He told Esau to take his weapons, bow and quiver, and go hunt game for him.
He asked Esau to prepare the tasty food he loved and bring it so he could eat and bless him before he died.
Rebekah was listening as Isaac spoke to Esau, and when Esau went out to hunt game for his father.
Rebekah said to her son Jacob that she had heard Isaac tell Esau.
To bring game and prepare tasty food so he could bless him before the LORD before he died.
She told Jacob to obey her voice in what she was about to command him.
She told him to go to the flock and bring her two good young goats so she could make tasty food for his father, just as he liked.
He would take it to his father to eat, so he would bless him before he died.
Jacob told his mother that his brother Esau was hairy, but he was smooth-skinned.
He worried his father might feel him and think he was mocking him, bringing a curse instead of a blessing.
His mother said the curse would be on her, and told him to obey her and go get the goats.
He went and got them and brought them to his mother, who made the tasty food his father loved.
Rebekah took Esau's best garments, kept in the house, and put them on her younger son Jacob.
She covered his hands and the smooth part of his neck with the goatskins.
She put the tasty food and bread she had made into Jacob's hand.
He went to his father and said, my father; Isaac answered, here I am, which of my sons are you.
Jacob told his father he was Esau, his firstborn, and had done as he was told, asking him to eat the game so he could give his blessing.
Isaac asked how he had found it so quickly, and Jacob said the LORD his God had made it happen.
Isaac asked him to come near so he could feel him, to know whether he really was Esau or not.
Jacob went near and Isaac felt him and said the voice was Jacob's voice, but the hands were Esau's hands.
Isaac did not recognize him, because his hands were hairy like Esau's, so he blessed him.
He asked again if he really was his son Esau, and Jacob said he was.
Isaac asked him to bring the food so he could eat and then bless him, and he ate and drank the wine Jacob brought.
His father Isaac told him to come near and kiss him.
Jacob went and kissed him, and Isaac smelled his clothes and blessed him, saying the smell was like the smell of a field the LORD had blessed.
He blessed him with the dew of heaven, the richness of the earth, and abundant grain and wine.
He blessed him that peoples would serve him and nations bow down to him, that he would be lord over his brothers, and that those who cursed him would be cursed and those who blessed him blessed.
As soon as Isaac finished blessing Jacob, and Jacob had just left his father, Esau came in from hunting.
Esau too prepared tasty food and brought it to his father, asking him to eat his son's game and bless him.
Isaac asked who he was, and he said he was his son, his firstborn, Esau.
Isaac trembled violently and asked who had already hunted game and brought it, whom he had blessed before Esau came, a blessing that would stand.
When Esau heard, he cried out loudly and bitterly, and asked his father to bless him too.
Isaac said his brother had come deceitfully and taken away his blessing.
Esau said Jacob was rightly named, for he had cheated him twice, taking his birthright and now his blessing, and asked if any blessing was left for him.
Isaac answered that he had made Jacob lord over him and given him all his brothers as servants, and sustained him with grain and wine, so what could he do for Esau now.
Esau asked if his father had only one blessing, and begged him to bless him too, and wept aloud.
Isaac answered that Esau's dwelling would be away from the richness of the earth and the dew of heaven above.
He said Esau would live by the sword and serve his brother, but when he grew restless he would break his brother's yoke from his neck.
Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing, and said to himself that once his father's mourning days came, he would kill his brother Jacob.
Rebekah was told what Esau planned, so she sent for Jacob and warned him that his brother meant to kill him.
She told him to obey her, get up, and flee to her brother Laban in Haran.
He should stay there a while until his brother's fury cooled down.
Once Esau's anger settled and he forgot what Jacob had done, she would send for him to come back, not wanting to lose both sons in one day.
Rebekah told Isaac she was weary of life because of the Hittite women, and if Jacob married a local Hittite woman too, life would not be worth living.
